content: Hospital payment systems determine provider incentives and healthcare costs: (1) Fee-for-service - payment per individual service, incentivizes volume; (2) Diagnosis-Related Groups (DRG) - fixed payment per admission based on diagnosis, incentivizes efficiency and shorter stays (adopted by Medicare 1983, now used in >50 countries); (3) Bundled payments - single payment for episode of care including pre/post-operative care, aligns incentives across providers; (4) Capitation - fixed per-member-per-month payment regardless of utilization, incentivizes prevention but risks under-treatment; (5) Global budgets - fixed annual allocation for hospital, used in rural hospitals and some integrated systems.

Payment reform progression moves from volume to value: (1) DRG refinement - adding complication/comorbidity tiers, outcome adjustments; (2) Hospital Readmissions Reduction Program - penalties for excess readmissions (CMS reduced payments by up to 3% for high readmission rates); (3) Hospital Value-Based Purchasing - tying 2% of Medicare payments to quality scores; (4) Bundled Payment for Care Improvement (BPCI) - voluntary programs for joint replacement, cardiac surgery showing 5-10% savings without quality deterioration; (5) Accountable Care Organizations (ACOs) - shared savings if spending below benchmark with quality targets met. Medicare ACOs achieved $2.4 billion savings in 2022 while improving quality metrics.

Payment system comparisons show: DRG systems reduced average length of stay by 20-30% after adoption without increasing mortality. Bundled payments for joint replacement reduced episode costs by 8-12% through decreased post-acute care utilization (more home health, less skilled nursing). Capitation systems show lower specialist referral rates and imaging use but require robust quality monitoring to detect under-treatment. Global budget hospitals in Maryland achieved $548 million savings over 5 years with reduced readmissions and ED visits. Risk adjustment is critical - inadequate adjustment leads to cream-skimming (avoiding sicker patients) or upcoding (assigning higher-severity DRGs). CMS-HCC risk adjustment models explain ~15-20% of cost variation.
